sourceTitle: Getting started as a template creator on Marketplace

questions.question: Which payment partner is integrated directly with Notion Marketplace for native payments?

options.text: Stripe
options.explanation: Stripe is the payments partner mentioned for native Notion payments.

options.text: PayPal
options.explanation: PayPal is not integrated natively; you would need a third‑party setup.

options.text: Square
options.explanation: Square is not referenced as a payment option in the guide.

options.text: Google Pay
options.explanation: Google Pay is not part of the Marketplace payment flow.

questions.question: Where does a creator build their Marketplace profile?

options.text: notion.so/profile
options.explanation: The guide specifies that the creator profile is crafted at https://www.notion.so/profile.

options.text: notion.so/settings
options.explanation: Settings is for account preferences, not the creator profile.

options.text: notion.so/marketplace
options.explanation: Marketplace is where listings appear, not where you edit your profile.

options.text: notion.so/dashboard
options.explanation: Dashboard shows activity; it does not host the creator profile page.

questions.question: Which feature helps protect a template’s content from being redistributed by others?

options.text: Access locking
options.explanation: Access locking is described as a way to protect templates from redistribution.

options.text: Two‑factor authentication
options.explanation: 2FA secures the creator’s account, not the template itself.

options.text: Public sharing link
options.explanation: A public link would actually make duplication easier.

options.text: Template versioning
options.explanation: Versioning tracks changes but does not prevent redistribution.

questions.question: Why might a creator prefer Notion’s native payment system over a third‑party tool?

options.text: It provides built‑in analytics and refund handling within Notion.
options.explanation: The guide states that only native payments give access to analytics and refunds directly in Notion.

options.text: It guarantees a higher payout percentage than third‑party processors.
options.explanation: Payout percentages are not discussed as a benefit of native payments.

options.text: It allows creators to receive payments in any cryptocurrency.
options.explanation: Cryptocurrency support is not mentioned for Notion payments.

options.text: It eliminates the need for a Stripe account entirely.
options.explanation: Native payments actually use Stripe as the backend; a Stripe account is still required.

questions.question: What happens to a buyer’s workspace when a refund is processed for a purchased template?

options.text: All instances of the template are removed, including pages, databases, and buttons.
options.explanation: The article explains that refunds strip the buyer’s workspace of every element of the template.

options.text: Only the main page of the template is deleted, leaving sub‑pages intact.
options.explanation: Refund removal is comprehensive, not limited to a single page.

options.text: The buyer receives a credit but the template remains unchanged.
options.explanation: Refunds actively delete the template content, not just issue a credit.

options.text: The template is archived for future reference but stays in the workspace.
options.explanation: Archiving is not part of the refund process described.

questions.question: How can a creator responsibly use email addresses collected during template purchases?

options.text: Send periodic newsletters with tips, while always providing an easy unsubscribe option.
options.explanation: The guide encourages community‑building emails but stresses respecting preferences and offering unsubscribe.

options.text: Sell the email list to third‑party marketers for additional revenue.
options.explanation: Selling email lists violates Marketplace rules and user trust.

options.text: Only email customers once to confirm the purchase, then never contact them again.
options.explanation: One‑time confirmation is allowed but the guide suggests ongoing engagement is okay if preferences are respected.

options.text: Automatically enroll them in paid advertising campaigns without consent.
options.explanation: Automatic enrollment without consent breaches the communication‑preference policy.

questions.question: Which Marketplace resource is most useful for a new creator to understand how successful listings are structured?

options.text: Browsing top duplicated templates and using the filter/sort options.
options.explanation: The guide recommends exploring duplicated templates to see what works in titles, descriptions, and visuals.

options.text: Reading the Notion API documentation.
options.explanation: API docs are unrelated to listing structure.

options.text: Viewing the Notion mobile app tutorial.
options.explanation: The mobile tutorial does not focus on Marketplace listings.

options.text: Checking the Notion community forum for unrelated topics.
options.explanation: While community forums are helpful, the guide specifically highlights duplicated templates for structural insight.

questions.question: What are the three main steps a creator must complete before listing their first template?

options.text: Create a creator profile, set up (or verify) payment processing, and craft a detailed listing with title, description, categories, and media.
options.explanation: These are the exact steps outlined: profile, payment verification, and the listing itself.

options.text: Publish a blog post, join the creator community, and submit a support ticket.
options.explanation: These actions are optional or unrelated to the core listing process.

options.text: Design a logo, register a domain, and create a YouTube channel.
options.explanation: While branding can help, they are not required steps for listing.

options.text: Complete a Notion certification, attend a live webinar, and obtain a Google Analytics account.
options.explanation: Certification and webinars are beneficial but not mandatory for the first listing.

questions.question: How does access locking differ from simply setting a template as private, and what impact does it have on redistribution?

options.text: Access locking encrypts the template’s content and prevents any duplication after purchase, whereas a private setting only hides the template from public view.
options.explanation: Access locking protects against redistribution but does not encrypt; private merely hides the listing.

options.text: Access locking is a Marketplace feature that actively restricts the buyer’s ability to copy or share the template’s pages, while a private setting only controls who can view the listing before purchase.
options.explanation: The guide explains that access locking protects content from being redistributed, unlike privacy which only limits visibility pre‑sale.

options.text: Both access locking and private settings perform the same function; there is no difference.
options.explanation: The article distinguishes the two: private controls visibility, access locking controls post‑purchase redistribution.

options.text: Private settings automatically apply access locking to every template, making the two interchangeable.
options.explanation: Private does not trigger access locking; they are separate controls.

questions.question: If a creator opts to use a third‑party payment tool instead of Notion’s native Stripe integration, what limitations will they face, and how might this affect their business operations on Marketplace?

options.text: They will lose access to Notion’s built‑in analytics and automated refund handling, meaning they must manage refunds manually and cannot track sales performance within Notion.
options.explanation: The guide states that third‑party tools lack analytics and refund capabilities that are provided by the native Stripe integration.

options.text: Their templates will be automatically removed from Marketplace until they switch back to native payments.
options.explanation: Templates remain listed; the limitation is on analytics and refunds, not removal.

options.text: They will be unable to receive any payouts at all, as Notion only processes payments through Stripe.
options.explanation: Payouts are still possible via the third‑party tool; the issue is lack of integrated features.

options.text: Customers will be unable to purchase their templates because Notion blocks non‑Stripe transactions.
options.explanation: Customers can still purchase using external processors; the limitation is on creator‑side analytics and refund management.
